text: The video shows the duodenum loosely attached to the infundibulum and fundus of the gallbladder, with visible chronic adhesions, fat, and inflammation indicative of acute on chronic cholecystitis.
The surgeon uses the elbow of the robotic arm number four to gently nudge the left lobe of the liver out of the way, ensuring better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon observes significant inflammation around the gallbladder, with a stone visibly lodged in its neck. The gallbladder exhibits extensive cholelithiasis.

The surgeon observes the pulsating hepatic artery, which is situated close to the surgical field. After administering additional ICG with a flush, the cystic artery is illuminated, facilitating a straightforward arterial dissection.
The surgeon examines a duct, attempting to distinguish it from a bile duct, while noting the limited visibility provided by the ICG fluorescence.

The surgeon uses the elbow of the robotic instrument to nudge the left lobe of the liver out of the way, revealing the gallbladder. The cystic duct is clearly visible, with small crossing vessels nearby, as the surgeon cleans the area to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon works on the cystic plate, encountering smaller vessels and visible pericholecystic fluid, indicative of prolonged inflammation and pain.

The surgeon carefully navigates the inflamed area, attempting to distinguish between the gallbladder and the cystic portion of the hepatic plate. The lack of a clear dissection plane is evident, and the surgeon proceeds slowly, following the path of inflammation.
